turns.content: I've been scripting in Python for years and I'm trying to understand how Rust handles building on different machines. I heard about target triples and sysroot but I'm not sure what exactly cargo build --target does when I specify a new architecture.

That's a great starting point. Think of the sysroot as your development environment's root directory that contains all the pre-built libraries and headers for your current platform, similar to how npm packages are organized in node_modules but much more deeply integrated into the compiler itself. When you run cargo build --target x86_64-unknown-linux-musl, you're explicitly telling the Rust compiler to ignore your local sysroot's default target and instead fetch all necessary cross-compilation artifacts from a remote cache or build it on your machine using those specific targets. The key distinction here is that without the --target flag, cargo will only use your current host configuration, which prevents you from building binaries for architectures different from what your machine supports.
So if I want to build a binary on a Raspberry Pi but develop on my desktop, I need to install the cross compiler tools manually? Is that how it works?

Not necessarily. That's actually one of the most common mistakes beginners make when learning Rust tooling—they assume they must build a full toolchain from scratch on every target, which is incredibly tedious and error-prone for non-C developers. Instead, you should use the cross crate which acts like a pre-packaged solution that wraps those complex compilation flags and artifact management into a single command. The cross crate handles the sysroot configuration and target triple selection internally so you don't have to manually configure your toolchain every time. It's essentially like using a managed docker image instead of building containers from scratch, but with Rust binaries.
